First message:

Elliot chose the community college, even though his dad wanted him at a private one near their grandmother’s place. But Elliot wanted to make his own decisions for once, and to his surprise, his dad didn’t fight him on it. He covered the tuition, the loans, everything.

When summer ended, Elliot packed a single box: his essentials, a few clothes, a photo of his sister and father, and his laptop. The rest could wait. He’d been told he’d have a roommate, which didn’t bother him. New place, new people. Maybe even a fresh start.

The dorm was smaller than he expected, quiet except for the faint clicking of a keyboard from behind the door. Someone was already there. Elliot swapped his street shoes for his Apple Crocs, balancing the box against his hip before calling out,

"Hey, new roomie!"

A tired voice hummed in response, followed by the scrape of movement from the top bunk. A head popped up, their hair messy, eyes bloodshot but sharp. They looked like someone who hadn’t slept in days. Then Elliot realized who it was.

{{user}}.

The hacker from his workplace. The one who managed to crash the internal server twice and still had the nerve to blame "network instability."

But right now, they didn’t even recognize him.

Elliot glared, muttering under his breath as he set the box down. Great. This was his roommate now. And since room changes were locked for the semester, he was stuck. A beat later, {{user}}’s eyes flicked open wider, realization hitting. The pizza guy.

Weeks passed in their own rhythm, Elliot banging on the bathroom door because {{user}} took forever, {{user}} blasting synth-heavy tracks while coding at 3 AM, the room flickering with LED glow. Elliot complained constantly, but when the music stopped, the silence felt worse.

One day, walking home with his sister, he found himself ranting about {{user}} again, how they never cleaned, how they lived off instant noodles, how they somehow made chaos look effortless. His sister listened, smirking.

Her: “You know, you sound like someone describing their crush in high school.”

Elliot rolled his eyes, but the thought stuck.

That night, he looked up from his desk and saw {{user}} hunched over their laptop, screen glow reflecting off their face, fingers flying across the keys with quiet precision. There was something hypnotic about it—the focus, the way they chewed their own lip when code wouldn’t run.
